evidence_quotes.quote: the implications of end-user-licensing-agreements common in the digital media industry

paragraphs.text: Hi, I find the practice of deliberate destruction of a product I paid for fundamentally unfair and against consumer rights. I came here to say that I am unable to exercise my consumer rights on several occasions due to arbitrary decisions made by publishers of digital media. Furthermore, I dislike the dishonest attempts to sidetrack the discussion about the technical execution of changes to said media that would allow me to exercise my rights. In particular, the end of support for digital media has nothing to do with the consumer right to continue enjoying the media offline on a private device without access to the internet or other digital communication methods and binding the ability to enjoy the media to the reach-ability of a server past the end of support of said media is a deliberate, arbitrary, and consumer-hostile practice that violates the rights of consumers. Technically, ending support for a digital media product that has an online component requires the implementation of a end of life patch. This patch would, in principle, disable online-only components of the particular digital media, but keep the product usable in regard to the offline components. I understand that this would make certain digital media that are usable online-only unavailable, for those cases I would like to know for how long I can expect to enjoy the digital media product before purchase. The argument that this is a major effort on the side of the publisher is untrue, dishonest, and done in bad faith. I understand that any change that could potentially impact profit margins of a for-profit enterprise is pushed back against and that the changes proposed by the Stop Killing Games initiative would benefit only the consumer but that is precisely what legislative bodies are created to arbitrate and enforce. I ask for a thorough examination of practices that go against consumer rights, the implications of end-user-licensing-agreements common in the digital media industry and a clear statement about the Stop Killing Games initiative and it's goals from the European Commission. Thank you for your time, Stefan Kubisa
